sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Traumatic Brain Injury (TBI) is a significant cause of morbidity and mortality worldwide. It can result from external forces leading to damage to the brain. The severity of TBI can vary from mild concussions to severe, life-threatening brain damage. Early identification, prompt management, and effective rehabilitation are essential for improving outcomes in patients with TBI.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Altered level of consciousness (confusion, agitation, drowsiness, unresponsiveness)
- Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS) less than 15
- Loss of consciousness (even transient)
- Vomiting (especially repeated)
- Severe or worsening headache
- Amnesia (antegrade or retrograde)
- Focal neurological deficits (e.g., limb weakness, facial droop, slurred speech)
- Unequal or unreactive pupils, sensitivity to light or noise
- Seizures
- Signs of skull fracture (periorbital ecchymosis, mastoid bruising, CSF leak from nose/ears)
- Penetrating head injury or visible scalp lacerations
- Bleeding from ears or nose
- Difficulty sleeping or staying asleep

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Hypoxia and hypotension worsen traumatic brain injury outcomes and should be aggressively avoided
- Hyperventilation should be reserved for signs of impending herniation and is not routine care
- Anticoagulant or antiplatelet therapy increases bleeding risk
- Head-up positioning may help venous drainage, but do not compromise perfusion in hypovolaemia

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Ensure scene safety and control catastrophic bleeding
- Primary Survey with spinal consideration, using jaw thrust rather than head tilt when needed
- Support airway and ventilation and administer oxygen to avoid hypoxaemia
- Record glucose, pupils, GCS, and other vital signs early and repeat often
- Apply spinal precautions when indicated and prioritise rapid evacuation
- Use head elevation only when it does not worsen haemodynamic status

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Consider advanced airway management if ventilation or airway protection is failing
- Establish IV or IO access and avoid hypotension with judicious isotonic fluid use
- Consider hypertonic saline 3% (HTS) for signs of impending transtentorial herniation - indicated when ANY of the following are present with GCS ≤8:
  - Fixed or unequally dilated pupils
  - Unilateral neurological signs
  - Cushing's triad (hypertension + bradycardia + irregular respirations)
  - GCS deterioration ≥2 points during care
  - Dose: 5 mL/kg IV/IO over 10–20 minutes (ASMA consult required for paediatrics)
  - HTS must be infused via a separate line from packed red blood cells

- Treat hypoglycaemia if present and apply cardiac monitoring
- Consider tranexamic acid in eligible patients within the protocol time window
- Provide analgesia, antiemetic therapy, and sedation within scope while avoiding deterioration in ventilation or perfusion

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Primary injury is generally irreversible therefore the aim of treatment is to prevent secondary injury by supporting cerebral haemodynamics and metabolism. Hypoxia and hypotension are among the most important preventable secondary insults
- Seizures
- Combativeness or agitation
- Clinical deterioration
- Death
- NICE head injury guidance advises considering TXA as soon as possible and within 2 hours of injury for eligible patients with moderate or severe traumatic brain injury
